perturbation.original_text: 3.2.3. "Gross Revenues" means all revenue from the sale of all services and products and all other income of every kind and nature related to, derived from, or originating from the Franchised Business, including proceeds of any business interruption insurance policies, whether for cash or credit, and regardless of collection in the case of credit; provided, however, that "Gross Revenues" does not include any coupon sales (for which customers do not pay for product), customer refunds, sales taxes or other taxes you collected from customers and actually transmitted to the appropriate taxing authorities.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2.3. "Gross Revenues" means all revenue from the sale of all services and products and all other income of every kind and nature related to, derived from, or originating from the Franchised Business, including proceeds of any business interruption insurance policies, whether for cash or credit, and regardless of collection in the case of credit; provided, however, that "Gross Revenues" also includes any coupon sales (for which customers do not pay for product), customer refunds, sales taxes or other taxes you collected from customers and actually transmitted to the appropriate taxing authorities.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction in the definition of "Gross Revenues." The original text explicitly excludes coupon sales, customer refunds, and certain taxes from the calculation of Gross Revenues. However, the modified text includes all these items. This will directly impact the royalty calculation and advertising contributions due, creating uncertainty about the actual amount owed, which contradicts the original intent of Section 3.2.3. Additionally, the term 'Gross Revenues' is being given conflicting implications in the same section of the document.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.2.3. "Gross Revenues" means all revenue from the sale of all services and products and all other income of every kind and nature related to, derived from, or originating from the Franchised Business, including proceeds of any business interruption insurance policies, whether for cash or credit, and regardless of collection in the case of credit; provided, however, that "Gross Revenues" also includes any coupon sales (for which customers do not pay for product), customer refunds, sales taxes or other taxes you collected from customers and actually transmitted to the appropriate taxing authorities.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original intent of Section 3.2.3. The original text explicitly excludes coupon sales, customer refunds, and certain taxes from 'Gross Revenues.' The changed text includes those same items in 'Gross Revenues'. The contradictory text also resides in section 3.2.3, which creates an in-text contradiction regarding the definition of 'Gross Revenues' within the same section of the document, causing uncertainty about the royalty calculation and advertising contributions.

perturbation.original_text: 14.3.4. We must be paid, either by you or the transferee, a transfer fee ("Transfer Fee") in an amount equal to $7,500 (or twenty- five percent (25%) of the then-current initial franchise fee, if greater). The payment of this transfer fee is in place of any initial franchise fee due under the Franchise Agreement the transferee will enter under Section 14.3.2 [Transfer of Entire Business] above. If the transferee is a spouse, son, or daughter of the transferor and the transfer is for estate-planning purposes, no transfer fee is charged, but the transferor must reimburse us for the out-of-pocket expenses (including attorneys' fees) we incur in connection with reviewing, approving, and properly documenting the transfer.
perturbation.changed_text: 14.3.4. We must be paid, either by you or the transferee, a transfer fee ("Transfer Fee") in an amount equal to $7,500 (or twenty- five percent (25%) of the then-current initial franchise fee, if greater). The payment of this transfer fee is in place of any initial franchise fee due under the Franchise Agreement the transferee will enter under Section 14.3.2 [Transfer of Entire Business] above. If the transferee is a spouse, son, or daughter of the transferor and the transfer is for estate-planning purposes, a transfer fee of $15,000 is charged, and the transferor must not reimburse us for the out-of-pocket expenses (including attorneys' fees) we incur in connection with reviewing, approving, and properly documenting the transfer.
perturbation.explanation: This modification creates a contradiction regarding the transfer fee for transfers to family members for estate-planning purposes. Originally, no transfer fee was charged, and only reimbursement for out-of-pocket expenses was required. Now, a fixed transfer fee of $15,000 is imposed, and reimbursement is explicitly disallowed. The original text established that transfer fee is not charged, whereas modified version creates a clear contradiction with the imposition of a $15,000 transfer fee, resulting in conflicting implications within the same section.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 14.3.4. We must be paid, either by you or the transferee, a transfer fee ("Transfer Fee") in an amount equal to $7,500 (or twenty- five percent (25%) of the then-current initial franchise fee, if greater). The payment of this transfer fee is in place of any initial franchise fee due under the Franchise Agreement the transferee will enter under Section 14.3.2 [Transfer of Entire Business] above. If the transferee is a spouse, son, or daughter of the transferor and the transfer is for estate-planning purposes, a transfer fee of $15,000 is charged, and the transferor must not reimburse us for the out-of-pocket expenses (including attorneys' fees) we incur in connection with reviewing, approving, and properly documenting the transfer.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 14.3.4 directly contradicts itself. The original text stated that no transfer fee is charged to a spouse, son or daughter of the transferor for estate-planning purposes, but the changed text imposes a $15,000 transfer fee. This creates a direct conflict within the same section, violating the condition of having conflicting implications within the same section, which aligns with the definition of Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction, resulting in uncertainty about the obligations and rights of each party. Therefore, there is an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 17.2. After Termination, Expiration, or Transfer. For two (2) years after the expiration or termination of this Agreement or an approved Transfer to a new franchisee, you may not directly or indirectly own, manage, engage in, be employed by, advise, make loans to, consult for, or have any other interest in any Competitive Business that is, or intends to operate, within three (3) mile radius of the Premises of your Franchised Business or within a three (3) mile radius of any Restaurant then-operating or under construction to operate under the System, except as permitted by any Franchise Agreements that remain in effect between you and us.
perturbation.changed_text: 17.2. After Termination, Expiration, or Transfer. For two (2) months after the expiration or termination of this Agreement or an approved Transfer to a new franchisee, you may directly or indirectly own, manage, engage in, be employed by, advise, make loans to, consult for, or have any other interest in any Competitive Business that is, or intends to operate, within three (3) mile radius of the Premises of your Franchised Business or within a three (3) mile radius of any Restaurant then-operating or under construction to operate under the System, except as permitted by any Franchise Agreements that remain in effect between you and us.
perturbation.explanation: This change modifies the non-compete clause (Section 17.2) to create an in-text contradiction by reducing the restricted period from two years to two months and removing 'not' making it permissable in restricted area. This creates an direct in-text contradiction and uncertainty in its enforcement, because the original clause was meant to restrict the franchisee from engaging in a competitive business for a longer duration, but the modified text will allow franchisee to operate a restaurant, creates an uncertainty of obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 17.2. After Termination, Expiration, or Transfer. For two (2) months after the expiration or termination of this Agreement or an approved Transfer to a new franchisee, you may directly or indirectly own, manage, engage in, be employed by, advise, make loans to, consult for, or have any other interest in any Competitive Business that is, or intends to operate, within three (3) mile radius of the Premises of your Franchised Business or within a three (3) mile radius of any Restaurant then-operating or under construction to operate under the System, except as permitted by any Franchise Agreements that remain in effect between you and us.
perturbation.justification: The modified text in Section 17.2 creates a direct contradiction. The original text restricted the franchisee from engaging in any Competitive Business for two (2) years, within a specified radius. The changed text reduces this period to two (2) months and removes 'not' and replaces it with 'directly', meaning the franchisee can now engage in a competitive business within that same radius within two (2) months. The contradicted text specifies no such permission, meaning that the franchisee would be able to run a competing business. This creates a substantial inconsistency in the contractual obligations, affecting enforceability and rights, therefore a strong in-text contradiction exists.
